title: What Emberline does
text: Emberline is a wildfire early-detection network built around fixed sensor towers that pair long-wave infrared imaging with visible-spectrum optical cameras to catch smoke plumes before they are visible to the unaided eye from the ground. No single sensing modality is trusted alone, since infrared or optical readings by themselves produce too many false alarms from dust, fog, or agricultural burning to support automatic dispatch. Towers sit along ridgelines and other fire-prone corridors, each covering a wedge of terrain that deliberately overlaps its nearest neighbors, so a genuine plume is very likely to be seen by more than one tower at once. That overlap is the network's primary defense against false positives, since cross-tower agreement is required before an alert reaches a human dispatcher. Emberline's current platform release, version 5.2, also folds in satellite-derived hotspot data, giving dispatch centers a combined ground-and-orbital picture of fire activity across the network's coverage area.

title: Tower hardware specification
text: Each Emberline tower carries an uncooled microbolometer array rated at 640 by 480 pixels, operating in the 8 to 14 micron long-wave infrared band with a noise-equivalent temperature difference of 50 millikelvin. A co-mounted 20-megapixel daytime optical camera sits on the same pan-tilt mount, slaved to the infrared unit's bearing so both sensors always frame the same wedge of terrain. Towers draw an average of 45 watts, supplied by a 320-watt solar panel and a 14-day battery reserve sized for extended overcast conditions. Backhaul runs over a 900 MHz mesh radio link, averaging 3 hops to the nearest fiber uplink point. As of the most recent network status report, 1,240 towers are in active service, spaced roughly 14 kilometers apart along covered corridors.

title: Plume detection algorithm
text: Emberline's plume detector works by differencing successive infrared frames to isolate slow, expanding thermal anomalies from the static background of terrain and vegetation. A candidate anomaly is passed to a texture classifier trained to distinguish the diffuse, expanding shape of smoke from the sharper edges of cloud shadow, dust, or vehicle exhaust. The classifier scores each candidate using both the infrared signature and the co-registered optical frame, since smoke's visible-band texture changes in a way heat alone does not capture. Wind data from the nearest weather station is folded in to predict how a genuine plume should drift between frames, letting the detector discard anomalies that fail to move the way real smoke would under current conditions. Only candidates that pass all three checks, thermal, textural, and drift-consistent, are forwarded to the cross-tower confirmation stage described elsewhere in this specification.

title: False-positive rate by detector version
text:
Suppressing false positives has been the dominant engineering focus across Emberline's platform history, and each major version measurably reduced the false-positive rate against a fixed validation set of archived tower footage.

| Version | Released | False-positive rate | Confirmation method |
|---|---|---|---|
| v2.0 | 2019 | 38% | single-tower threshold |
| v3.1 | 2021 | 19% | optical-infrared fusion |
| v4.0 | 2023 | 11% | two-tower triangulation |
| v5.2 | 2025 | 6.4% | three-tower plus satellite fusion |

The jump from version 4.0 to 5.2 came almost entirely from adding satellite hotspot corroboration as a fourth independent signal rather than from any change to the underlying image classifier.

title: Satellite hotspot fusion
text: Emberline supplements ground-tower detections with hotspot data from a polar-orbiting satellite partnership, which revisits any given point in the coverage area roughly every 90 minutes with a thermal-anomaly pixel resolution of 375 meters. A satellite-flagged hotspot that falls within 2 kilometers of a tower's own candidate detection raises that detection's confirmation score sharply, often enough on its own to clear the alert threshold without waiting for a second ground tower. Satellite passes are far too infrequent to serve as a primary detection source, given the 90-minute revisit interval against fires that can double in size within 15 minutes under dry, windy conditions. Instead, the satellite feed functions purely as corroborating evidence, most valuable in the sparser stretches of the network where tower spacing exceeds the 18-kilometer nominal detection range for a small plume.

title: Confirmation-probability threshold
text: Emberline models cross-tower confirmation as a simple independence assumption to set its alert threshold. If a single tower's detector has a true-positive rate p and a false-positive rate q for a given candidate, requiring agreement from k of n overlapping towers before dispatch reduces the combined false-positive probability to roughly q^k, while the combined true-positive probability degrades much more slowly since p is kept deliberately high, around 0.94 per tower in the current model. Emberline requires k = 2 of the towers covering a given cell to agree, chosen because q^2 already pushes the modeled combined false-positive rate below 1%, while k = 3 would push median confirmation latency past the network's 5-minute dispatch-latency target in the sparser parts of the coverage area. The satellite feed is treated as a bonus signal that can substitute for the second tower when only one ground tower observes a candidate.

title: Tower commissioning checklist
text:
Field technicians follow a fixed commissioning sequence before a newly installed tower is admitted to the live detection network.
1. Verify solar charge controller output holds the battery above 90% state of charge for 48 continuous hours.
2. Align the infrared and optical sensor pair against three known landmarks and confirm bearing agreement within 0.2 degrees.
3. Run the standard smoke-simulation test cartridge and confirm the tower's detector flags it within 90 seconds.
4. Confirm mesh radio backhaul reaches the nearest fiber uplink within the expected 3-hop budget.
5. Register the tower's coverage wedge in the central confirmation model so overlapping neighbors recognize it immediately.
A tower that fails the smoke-simulation test at step three is held in a non-alerting monitoring mode until a technician revisits the site, rather than being pulled from the network entirely.

title: Dispatch alert latency
text: Median time from a genuine plume's first appearance in a tower's infrared frame to a confirmed alert reaching a dispatch center currently stands at 4.1 minutes across the network, with a 95th-percentile latency of 9.3 minutes in sparser coverage cells. Single-tower candidate flagging accounts for roughly 90 seconds of that median, with cross-tower or satellite confirmation adding another 90 to 150 seconds depending on how quickly a second signal arrives. The remaining latency is dispatch-center queuing and operator acknowledgment, which Emberline's own telemetry cannot reduce further since it sits outside the detection pipeline itself. The network's internal target is a 5-minute median end-to-end latency, a threshold set jointly with partner fire agencies based on typical plume-to-flame-front progression rates during dry, windy conditions.

title: FAQ: how fast does a tower flag a candidate plume?
text: A question that comes up often from partner agencies evaluating Emberline for a new region: how long does it take a single tower to flag a candidate plume once smoke first appears in its field of view? A: Single-tower candidate flagging accounts for roughly 150 seconds of that median, with cross-tower or satellite confirmation adding another 90 to 150 seconds depending on how quickly a second signal arrives. The median in question is the network's end-to-end alert latency, covered in the dispatch-latency section of this specification. Agencies sometimes assume the flagging figure includes confirmation time, which it does not; confirmation is a separate stage layered on top. New deployments in unusually dense vegetation cover have occasionally seen single-tower flagging run somewhat slower than this figure during early calibration, before local thresholds are tuned to the terrain.

title: Backhaul and mesh architecture
text: Rather than giving every tower a direct link to a dispatch center, Emberline routes tower telemetry and imagery through a mesh of radio relays that ultimately terminate at a small number of fiber uplink points scattered across the coverage area. A tower with no direct line of sight to an uplink point forwards its traffic through neighboring towers, which is why average hop count, rather than raw radio range, is the figure operators watch most closely when planning new deployments. This mesh design tolerates the loss of any single relay tower without severing connectivity for towers behind it, as long as an alternate path through a different neighbor exists. During planning for a new corridor, engineers deliberately place towers so that no detection-capable tower depends on a single relay path to reach an uplink, trading a modest increase in tower density for meaningfully better backhaul resilience during a fire event, which is exactly when a relay tower is most likely to be damaged or lose power.

title: Coverage and deployment statistics
text: Emberline's coverage area currently spans approximately 210,000 square kilometers across two continents, following the network's 2022 expansion from its original western United States footprint into fire-prone regions of southern Europe. The network operates 1,240 towers as of the most recent status report, with a further 96 towers under construction or awaiting commissioning. Average tower spacing sits at 14 kilometers, though spacing narrows to as little as 9 kilometers in the highest fire-risk corridors and widens to 22 kilometers in lower-priority buffer zones. The network's founding organization began pilot deployment in 2018 with 40 towers across a single fire-prone corridor, reaching 500 towers by the end of 2021 before the international expansion began.

title: Dispatch latency by coverage density
text:
Confirmation latency varies substantially with how densely a given cell of the network is covered, since sparser cells more often depend on the slower satellite corroboration path rather than a fast second ground tower.

| Coverage density | Median confirmation latency | Primary confirmation source |
|---|---|---|
| Dense (spacing under 10 km) | 95 seconds | second ground tower |
| Standard (10-16 km) | 140 seconds | second ground tower |
| Sparse (over 16 km) | 310 seconds | satellite hotspot feed |

Sparse-cell latency remains within the network's overall 5-minute dispatch target only because single-tower flagging in those cells is otherwise fast, leaving enough headroom for the slower satellite-dependent confirmation step.

title: International detection research
text: Wildfire sensor-network research draws on engineering traditions well beyond the English-language literature most Emberline engineers cite first. Papadopoulou's 2017 study, Ανίχνευση καπνού από δίκτυα αισθητήρων σε ορεινές περιοχές, examines smoke detection from ridge-mounted sensor networks in mountainous terrain closely resembling Emberline's own tower placement strategy, and is cited directly in the plume-detection algorithm's design notes. Konstantinidis's later work on multi-sensor fusion thresholds for false-alarm suppression in wildland fire monitoring informed the independence-assumption model behind the network's confirmation-probability threshold. Emberline's engineering team credits Greek fire-monitoring research specifically for the early insight that infrared and optical signals fail in largely uncorrelated ways, which is what makes combining them worthwhile in the first place.

title: Satellite corroboration weighting
text: When a satellite-flagged hotspot substitutes for a second ground tower, Emberline weights it using a discount factor rather than treating it as fully equivalent evidence. The combined confirmation score is computed as S = w1 * c1 + w2 * c2, where c1 is the primary tower's candidate confidence, c2 is the corroborating signal's confidence, and w2 is set to 0.7 for a satellite hotspot versus 1.0 for a genuine second ground tower, reflecting the coarser 375-meter pixel resolution and up-to-90-minute staleness of orbital data. An alert dispatches once S crosses a fixed threshold of 1.5, a value chosen so that two full-weight ground towers alone (1.0 + 1.0) clear it comfortably, while a single tower plus satellite corroboration (1.0 + 0.7) clears it only when the primary tower's own confidence is already reasonably high. This weighting keeps satellite-only corroboration from single-handedly triggering dispatch when ground-tower confidence is marginal.

title: Changelog: v3.1 backhaul radio migration
text: Version 3.1 release notes, expanded here for engineers migrating older tower firmware. This release completed the fleet-wide radio migration: backhaul runs over a 2400 MHz mesh radio link, averaging 3 hops to the nearest fiber uplink point, replacing a mix of legacy hardware from the network's earliest pilot deployments. Field crews reported noticeably better link stability in dense canopy cover after the migration, attributed to better multipath handling in the newer radio chipset rather than the frequency change itself. The release also introduced automatic hop-count rebalancing, letting a tower silently reroute through a different neighbor if its primary uplink path degraded, without requiring a technician visit.

title: Weather integration statistics
text: Emberline ingests observations from 340 partner weather stations across its coverage area, refreshed at 10-minute intervals, to feed the wind-drift consistency check used during plume classification. Stations average 6.8 kilometers from their nearest tower, close enough that wind readings remain locally representative for the drift model's purposes. During the 2024 fire season, the drift-consistency check discarded an estimated 14% of otherwise-plausible candidate detections as inconsistent with observed wind conditions, the single largest false-positive filter in the detection pipeline by volume. Coverage gaps in weather-station density are concentrated in the network's newer southern European corridors, where station buildout has not yet caught up with tower buildout.

title: Internal audit finding: tower count at 2022 expansion
text: Finding recorded during the network's most recent internal engineering audit, filed under deployment-history accuracy. The audit team cross-checked historical status reports against archived commissioning records and flagged a discrepancy in one internal summary, which stated: The network operates 1,340 towers as of the most recent status report, with a further 96 towers under construction or awaiting commissioning. The audit traced the 1,340 figure to a transcription error rather than any actual fleet count, since archived commissioning records support 1,240. The audit recommends the summary document be corrected to cite the accurate figure, and the correction has been queued for the next documentation review cycle.

title: Incident postmortem: the 2023 Bluestem Creek false-dispatch cluster
text: On the afternoon of August 14th, 2023, six Emberline towers along the Bluestem Creek corridor flagged candidate plumes within a 40-minute window, four of which cleared the network's confirmation threshold and triggered dispatch alerts to the regional fire agency before any were identified as false positives. Investigation traced the root cause to an unusually large agricultural burn roughly 30 kilometers outside the corridor, whose smoke plume drifted directly along the region's prevailing wind corridor and passed close enough to several towers to register strong infrared and optical signatures nearly indistinguishable from wildfire smoke at the classifier's current sensitivity settings. The wind-drift consistency check, designed to filter exactly this kind of false positive, failed to flag the agricultural smoke because its drift pattern happened to closely match what the model expected from a genuine plume originating near the corridor itself, rather than one drifting in from well outside it. Cross-tower confirmation, normally the network's strongest defense, worked against detection accuracy in this incident specifically because the smoke was visible to multiple towers simultaneously, satisfying the very agreement threshold meant to build confidence in a genuine detection. The regional fire agency dispatched two engine crews and a spotter aircraft before ground confirmation revealed no active fire, at an estimated response cost later reported internally as roughly 40,000 dollars across the four false dispatches. The immediate remediation added a known agricultural-burn registry that partner farms can voluntarily register seasonal burns against, letting the detection pipeline down-weight candidates whose location and timing match a registered burn window. A longer-term fix, still in development as of the most recent engineering roadmap, adds a smoke-origin triangulation step that estimates a candidate plume's point of origin from parallax across multiple towers, distinguishing a plume originating well outside the corridor from one originating within it regardless of how the two might look from a single tower's perspective. Partner fire agencies were notified of the incident within 24 hours under the network's standing disclosure agreement, and the agricultural-burn registry shipped as an opt-in feature roughly six weeks later. No similar false-dispatch cluster of this scale has been recorded since the registry's introduction, though engineering leadership has been careful to note that a full fire season has not yet passed under the new system, and the registry only helps against registered burns rather than uncontrolled or unregistered agricultural fires nearby. Agency leadership stated that a false dispatch, while costly, remains preferable in their judgment to a missed genuine detection under comparable conditions, and the postmortem's authors flagged that tradeoff as a policy question rather than a purely technical one.

title: Current status and roadmap
text: Emberline's platform sits at version 5.2 as of the most recent release, with the smoke-origin triangulation feature developed after the Bluestem Creek incident slated for a version 6.0 release still in active testing. The network's operating consortium has publicly stated an intention to expand coverage into additional Mediterranean corridors over the next two fire seasons, pending funding commitments from partner agencies in the affected regions. Engineering priorities beyond triangulation include reducing sparse-cell confirmation latency, currently the network's weakest performance figure relative to its dispatch-latency target, and expanding the weather-station partnership network to close coverage gaps in newer corridors. None of these roadmap items are expected to change the network's core three-signal confirmation architecture, which the engineering team considers validated by several fire seasons of production operation despite the occasional high-profile false-dispatch incident.
